title: Chocolate on Carpet
stainChemistry: Classification: Combination. Chocolate is a complex emulsion of cocoa solids (tannins + pigments), cocoa butter (fat), milk solids (protein), and sugar. The protein component (milk casein) bonds to carpet fiber through hydrogen bonding. The fat component (cocoa butter, ~50% of chocolate) is lipophilic and grabs synthetic fibers tenaciously. Treatment sequence matters — wrong order can set one component while removing another.
whyThisWorks: Chocolate combines several stain types at once — each needing a different approach. Treat the fat first with dry-side solvent, then the protein with digestant, then the residual color with oxidizer.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply Tannin to address the color component of the chocolate stain. Tamp gently.

2. Apply POG to dissolve the oil and fat portion of the chocolate stain. If POG alone doesn't resolve the oily residue, switch to amyl acetate. Never use amyl acetate on acetate fabrics. Blot with clean absorbent cloth.
3. Apply Protein to the chocolate stain and tamp gently with a spotting brush. Protein must be addressed — heat and oxidizers set protein stains permanently. Work from the edge toward the center.
4. Apply H₂O₂ 6% to oxidize any remaining chocolate color. Check progress every few minutes. Test on colored fabrics first.
5. Flush with NSD to remove all spotting agent residue and loosened stain material. Rinse thoroughly with cool water.
6. Apply diluted acetic acid (1:10) to neutralize any remaining alkaline residue. Blot dry with clean absorbent cloth.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold — applying heat immediately or using hot water sets both protein and fat deeper into carpet fiber
warnings: Applying heat immediately or using hot water — sets both the protein and fat deeper into the fiber
Scraping while chocolate is warm — melted chocolate smears and spreads the stain
Using only Protein without addressing fat — chocolate is both protein and fat, both components need treatment
Not letting enzymes dwell long enough — minimum 10 minutes for effective digestion

steps: 1. Scrape or wipe away any solid material. Use cold water.
2. Mix mild dish soap into cold water. Blot the solution onto the stain.
4. Blot with a clean white cloth. Repeat if needed.
5. Blot with a cloth dampened with plain water to rinse. Place a stack of paper towels over the spot and weigh them down. Let it dry completely.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Try an enzyme presoak followed by oxygen bleach. Apply and let it soak for 30–60 minutes. If the stain survives two rounds, call a professional carpet cleaner.
warnings: Always blot — never rub. Rubbing damages carpet fibers and spreads the stain.
Place paper towels over the wet spot and weigh them down to prevent wicking overnight.
Always scrape off solid material first — don't smear it around.

neverDo: Apply heat immediately or use hot water — sets protein and fat deeper into fiber
Scrape while chocolate is warm — spreads the stain
Rub instead of blot — damages carpet fibers
fiberSensitivities: On synthetic carpet: test all agents in an inconspicuous area first. Monitor for color change, surface dulling, or texture damage during treatment.

escalation.when: Stain remains after two full treatment cycles, or stain covers a large area
escalation.whatToTell: Chocolate stain on carpet, multi-component (protein, fat, tannin). Treated at home with NSD. Please use hot-water extraction with enzyme pre-treatment.

contextQuestions: Was the chocolate warm/melted or solid when the stain occurred?
Is the stain fresh or has it dried?
Has any cleaning attempt already been made?
Is this milk chocolate, dark chocolate, or white chocolate?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Warm/melted chocolate soaks deeper and is harder to remove. Dark chocolate has more tannin. Milk chocolate has more protein. White chocolate is mostly fat with minimal pigment. Prior cleaning attempts may have set components further.
